title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 21 — Cao Cao Discusses Heroes / Guan Yu Beheads Che Zhou
text:
“Who do you suggest?” askedDong ChengofMa Teng.

“TheShepherdofYu province,Liu Bei,” answeredMa Teng. “He is here and we ought to ask him to help.”

“Though he is an uncle ofthe Emperor, he is at present a partizan of our enemy, and he will not join.”

“But I saw something at the hunt,” saidMa Teng. “WhenCao Caoadvanced to acknowledge the congratulations due tothe Emperor,Xuande’s sworn brotherGuan Yuwas behind him, and grasped his sword as if to cut downCao Cao. However,Xuandesigned to him to hold his hand and he did. He would willingly destroyCao Cao, only he thinks his teeth and claws are too many. You must ask him and he will surely consent.”

HereWu Shuourged caution. “Do not go too fast,” said he. “Let us consider the thing most carefully.” And matters concluded, they dispersed.

On the next day, after dark,Dong Chengwent toLiu Bei’s lodging to discussthe decree. As soon as he was announced,Xuandecame to greet him and led him into a private room where they could talk freely.Guan YuandZhang Feijoined them.

“It must be something unusually important that has broughtUncleDong Chenghere this night,” observedLiu Bei.

“If I had ridden forth by daylight,Cao Caomight have suspected something, so I came by night,” explainedDong Cheng.

Wine was brought, in and while they were drinking,Dong Chengasked, “Why did you check your brother the other day at the hunt, when he was going to attackCao Cao?”

Xuandewas startled and asked, “How did you know?”

“Nobody else noticed, but I did.”

Xuandecould not prevaricate and said, “It was the presumption of the man that made my brother so angry; he could not help it.”

The visitor covered his face and wept.

“Ah,” saidDong Cheng, “if all the court ministers were likeGuan Yu, there would be no sighs for lack of tranquility.”

Xuandegrew concerned thatCao Caomay have sent his visitor to test him, so he cautiously replied, “Why would there be sighs for lack of tranquility whileCao Caois at the head of affairs?”

Dong Chengchanged color and rose from his seat.

“You, Sir, areHis Excellency’s uncle that I have shown you my inmost feelings. Why have you misled me?”

Liu Beiexplained, “Because I feared you might be misleading me. I had to test you.”

At thisDong Chengdrew out and sharedthe decree. Sorry and indignation welled up inXuandeas he read thethe Emperor’s sacred words.

Dong Chengthen produced the pledge. There were only six names to it:Dong Cheng, theGeneral of Chariots and Cavalry; Wang Zifu aSecretariat Minister of the Industry Bureau;Chong JitheCommandant of the Chang River Encampments;Wu Shuo, aGentleman Consultant;Wu ZilantheGeneral of Manifest Trust; andMa Teng, theGrand AdministratorofXiliang.

“Since you havea decreelike this, I cannot but do my share,” saidLiu Bei, and atDong Cheng’s request he added his name and signature to the others and handed it back.

“Now let us get but three more, ten in all, and we shall be ready to act.”

“You must move with great caution to keep this secret,” cautionedXuande.

The two talked until an early hour in the morning when the visitor left.

Now in order to putCao Caoquite off the scent that a plot against him was in progress,Liu Beibegan to devote himself to gardening, planting vegetables, and watering them with his own hands.Guan YuandZhang Feiventured to remonstrate with him for taking to such an occupation when great matters needed attention.

“The reason for this you may not know,” repliedLiu Bei.

And his brothers said no more.

One day, when the two brothers were absent,Xuandewas busy in his garden when two ofCao Cao’s generals,Xu ChuandZhang Liao, arrived with an escort, saying, “At the command of theAssisting Chancellor, you are to come at once.”

“What important affair is afoot?” askedXuandenervously.

“We know nothing: we were ordered to come and request your presence.”

All he could do was to follow.

WhenLiu Beiarrived,Cao Caomet him in a jovial state and laughed, saying, “That is a big business you have in hand at home.”

This remark madeLiu Beiturn the color of clay. ButCao Caotook him by the hand and led him straight to his private garden, saying, “The growth of vegetables that you are trying to learn is very difficult.”

Xuandebreathed a sigh of relief. “This is just a solace. I have nothing else with which to occupy my time.”

Cao Caosaid, “I happened to notice the green plums on the trees today and suddenly my thoughts went back to a year ago when we were thrashingZhang Xiu. We were marching through a parched district and every one was suffering from thirst. Suddenly I lifted my whip and pointing at something in the distance I said, ‘Look at those plum trees.’ The soldiers heard it and it made their mouths water. Now I owe something to the plums and we will pay it today. I ordered the servants to heat some wine and sent for you to join me.”

Xuandenow felt quite composed, and no longer suspected a sinister design. He went with his host to a small summer house, where the wine cups were already laid out and green plums filled the dishes. After a goblet of wine had been swallowed they sat down to a confidential talk and enjoyment of their wine.

As they drank the weather gradually changed, clouds gathering and threatening rain. The servants pointed out a mass of cloud that looked like a dragon hung in the sky. Both host and guest went to the window and leaned over the rail looking at it.

“Do you understand the evolutions of dragons?” askedCao Cao.

“Not in great detail,” repliedXuande.

“A dragon can assume any size; can rise in glory or hide from sight,“ explainedCao Cao. “Enlarged, it generates clouds and gushes mist. Diminished, it can scarcely hide a mustard stalk or conceal a shadow. Soaring, it can ascend to the empyrean. Below, it lurks in the uttermost depths of the ocean. This is the mid-spring season and the dragon chooses this moment for transformation, like men who would realize their ambition and overrun the world. In this respect, the dragon compares with the heroes of the age. You,Xuande, have traveled near and far, through all the lakes and rivers, and must know the heroes of our time. Please try to describe them for me.”

“How can a dullard like me know such things?”

“Set aside your modesty,” urgedCao Cao.

“Thanks to your kindly protection I have a post at court. But as to heroes I really do not know who they are.”

“You may not have looked upon their faces, but you have heard their names.”

“Yuan Shu, with his resources; is he one?”

His host laughed, “A rotting bone in a graveyard. I shall put him out of the way shortly.”

“Well,Yuan Shaothen. The highest offices of State have been held in his family for four generations and his clients are many. He is firmly posted inJi provinceand he commands the services of many able men. Surely he is one.”

“A bully, but a coward; he is fond of grandiose schemes, but is devoid of decision; he makes for great things but grudges the necessary toil. He loses sight of everything else in view of a little present advantage. He is not one.”

“There isLiu Jingsheng. He is renowned as a man of perfection, whose fame has spread on all sides. Surely he is a hero.”

“He is a mere semblance, a man of vain reputation. No; not he.”

“Sun Ceis a sturdy sort, the chief of all in the east. Is he a hero?”

“He has profited by his father’s reputation; he is no hero.”

“What ofLiu Zhang?”

“Though he is of the reigning family, he is nothing more than a watch dog. How could you make a hero of him?”

“What aboutZhang Xiu,Zhang Lu,Han Suiand all those?”

Cao Caoclapped his hands and laughed very loudly. “Paltry people like them are not worth mentioning.”

“With these exceptions I really know none.”

“Now heroes are men who cherish lofty designs in their bosoms and have plans to achieve them; they have all-embracing schemes and the whole world is at their mercy.”

“Who could live up to such a description?” askedXuande.

Cao Caopointed his finger first at his guest and then at himself, saying, “The only heroes in the world are you and I.”

Xuandegasped and the spoon and chopsticks rattled to the floor. Now just at that moment the storm burst with a tremendous peal of thunder and rush of rain.Xuandestooped down to recover the fallen articles, saying, “What a shock! and it was quite close.”

“What! are you afraid of thunder?” askedCao Cao.

Xuandereplied, “The wise man paled at a sudden peal of thunder or fierce gust of wind. Why should one not fear?”

Thus he glossed over the real fact, that it was the words he had heard that had so startled him.

Constrained to lodge in a tiger’s lair,

He played a waiting part,

But when Cao talked of breaking men,

Then terror gripped his heart.

But he cleverly used the thunder peal

As excuse for turning pale;

O quick to seize occasions thus!

He surely must prevail.

The shower had passed and there appeared two men rushing through the garden, both armed. In spite of the attendants they forced their way to the pavilion where sat the two friends. They wereGuan YuandZhang Fei.

The two brothers had been outside the city at archery practice whenCao Cao’s invitation had come so peremptorily. On their return they heard that two officers had arrived and led awayXuandetotheChancellor. They hastened to his palace and were told their brother was with his host in the grounds and they feared something had happened. So they rushed in. Now when they saw their brother quietly talking withCao Caoand enjoying a cup of wine, they took up their usual places and meekly stood waiting.

“Why did you come?” saidCao Cao.

“We heard that you, Sir, had invited our brother to a wine party and we came to amuse you with a little sword play,” said they.

“This is not a Hungmen banquet,” repliedCao Cao. “What use have we forXiang Zhuangand Xiang Bo of old?”

Xuandesmiled. The host ordered wine to be served to the two “Fan Kuai” to allay their excitement and, soon after, the three took their leave and returned homeward.

“We were nearly frightened to death,” saidGuan Yu.

The story of the dropped chopsticks was told. The two asked what their brother intended by his actions and he told them that his learning gardening was to convinceCao Caoof his perfect simplicity and the absence of any ambition. “But,” said he, “when he suddenly pointed to me as one of the heroes I was startled, for I thought he had some suspicions. Happily the thunder at that moment supplied the excuse I wanted.”

“Really you are very clever,” said they.

Next dayCao Caoagain invitedXuandeand while the two were drinking,Man Chong, who had been dispatched to find out whatYuan Shaowas doing, came to present his report.

He said, “Gongsun Zanhas been completely defeated byYuan Shao.”

“Do you know the details? I should like to know how,” interruptedLiu Bei.

“They were at war andGongsun Zansuffered the worst of it, so he acted on the defensive, building a high wall about his army and on that erecting a high tower, which he called theYijing Tower. Therein he placed all his grain and took up his own quarters. His fighting men passed in and out without ceasing, some going out to give battle, others returning to rest. One of them was surrounded and sent to askGongsun Zanto rescue him.Gongsun Zansaid, “If I rescue him, hereafter every one will want to be helped and will not exert himself.” So he did not go. This disgusted his men and many deserted to the enemy so that his army diminished. He sent letters to the capital to crave help, but the messenger was captured. He sent toZhang Yanto arrange with him for a joint attack and those letters with the plans also fell intoYuan Shao’s hands and the plans were adopted by his enemy, who gave the signals agreed upon. ThusGongsun Zanfell into an ambush, lost heavily and retreated into the city. There he was besieged and a subterranean passage was pierced into the tower where he lodged. The tower was set on fire andGongsun Zancould not escape. So he slew his wife and little ones and committed suicide. The flames destroyed the bodies of the whole family.

“Yuan Shaohas added the remnants of the vanquished army to his own and so become yet stronger. His brotherYuan Shuin Honan, however, has become so arrogant and cruel that the people have turned against him. Then he had sent to say he would yield the title of Emperor, which he had assumed, in favor of his brother. His brotherYuan Shaodemanded the seal also andYuan Shupromised to bring it in person. Now he has abandonedHuainanand is about to occupyHopei. If he succeeded, the two brothers will control adjoining districts and be dangerous.”

It was a sad story andXuanderemembered with sorrow that, in the days of success and prosperity, the dead chieftain had pushed his interest and shown him much kindness. Moreover he was anxious to know the fate ofZhao Zilong.

In his heart he thought, “What better chance am I likely to get of setting myself free?”

So he rose and said toCao Cao, “IfYuan Shugoes over to join his brother he will surely pass throughXu province. I beg you to give me an army with which to smite him on the way. That will finishYuan Shu.”

“Memorializethe Emperortomorrow and I will give you an army,” saidCao Cao.

So next dayXuandewent to an audience andCao Caogave him command of fifty thousand men, horse and foot, and sentZhu LingandLu Zhaowith him.

At parting withLiu Bei,the Emperorshed tears. As soon as he reached his lodging he set about preparations for immediate departure, returning his seal as General and preparing his weapons.Dong Chengwent out some distance on the road to bid him farewell.

“You must not mind my going; this journey will assuredly help on the scheme,” saidLiu Bei.

“Keep your mind fixed on that,” saidDong Cheng, “and never forget whatHis Excellencyrequires of us.”

They parted. Presently his brothers asked him why he was in such a hurry to get away.

Xuandereplied, “I have been a bird in a cage, a fish in a net. This is like the fish regaining the open sea and the bird soaring into the blue sky. I suffered much from the confinement.”

NowGuo JiaandCheng Yuhad been absent inspecting stores and supplies whenLiu Beileft. As soon as they heard of his expedition they went in to see their master, asking him why he had letLiu Beigo in command of an army.

“He is going to cut offYuan Shu.”

“Formerly, when he wasShepherdofYu province, we recommended that he should be put to death but you would not hear of it. Now you have given him an army. You have allowed the dragon to reach the sea, the tiger to return to the mountains. What control will you have in future?”

So spokeCheng YuandGuo Jiafollowed in the same strain.

“Even if you would not put him to death you need not have let him go. As the proverb says, ‘Relax opposition for one day and age-long harm ensues.’ You must admit the truth of this.”

Cao Caorecognized that these were prudent counsels so he sentXu Chuwith a half company and imperative orders to bringXuandeback again.

Liu Beiwas marching as rapidly as possible when he noticed a cloud of dust in the rear and remarked to his brothers, “Surely they are pursuing us.”

He halted and made a stockade and ordered his brothers to be in readiness, one on each flank. Presently the messenger arrived and found himself in the midst of an army ready for battle. He dismounted and entered the camp to speak withXuande.

“Sir, on what business have you come?” askedXuande.

“TheChancellorhas sent me to request you to return as he has further matters to discuss with you.”

“When a general has once taken the field even the royal command is of no effect. I bade farewell tothe Emperor, I receivedtheChancellor’s commands and there can be nothing further to talk about. You may return forthwith and take that as my reply.”

Xu Chuwas undecided on what action to take. He thought, “TheAssisting Chancellorcherishes his friendship withLiu Bei, and I have no orders to kill. I can only return with this reply and ask further instructions.” And so he left.

WhenXu Churelated what had transpired,Cao Caostill hesitated to take action.

“This refusal to return means enmity,” urgedCheng YuandGuo Jia.

“Still, two of my men are with him,” saidCao Cao. “He will not dare do anything unfriendly, I think. Beside, I sent him and I cannot go back on my own orders.”

SoXuandewas not pursued.

He took his arms, he fed his steed,

And fared forth willingly,

Intent to accomplish his King’s behest

Deep grayed on his memory.

At least he had broken out of his cage,

He heard not the tiger’s roar,

He had shaken the shackles from his feet,

As a dragon on high could soar.

As soon asMa Tengheard thatLiu Beihad set forth he reported that pressing business called him and marched back to his own district.

WhenXuandereachedXu provincetheShepherd,Che Zhou, came to meet him, and when the official banquet was over,Sun QianandMi Zhucame to visit him. Then he proceeded to his residence to greet his family.

Scouts were sent out to see whatYuan Shuwas doing. They came back with the intelligence that his arrogance had driven away his banditti allies, who had returned to their mountain fastnesses. His forces thus reduced he had written resigning the Imperial style he had assumed in favor of his brotherYuan Shao, who had at once commanded his presence. Thereupon he packed up the palace fittings he had had made, got the remnants of his army in order and marched west.

When he nearedXu province,Xuandeled fifty thousand men, joined byGuan YuandZhang Fei, along withZhu LingandLu Zhao, to meet him.Yuan Shusent outJi Lingto force a way through, ButZhang Feiopposed him and attacked without a parley. In the tenth bout he cut downJi Ling. The defeated soldiers fled in all directions.

ThenYuan Shucame up with his own army.Xuandeplaced his captains right and left, he himself being in the center, and so metYuan Shu. As soon as the enemy came nearXuandebegan to abuse him. “O rebellious one, and wicked, I have a command to destroy you. Yield, then, with good grace and so escape your punishment.”

“Base weaver of mats and mean maker of straw sandals, how dare you make light of me?” repliedYuan Shuand he gave the signal for an attack.

Xuanderetired and his captains from the flanks closed in. They smote the army ofYuan Shuuntil corpses littered the plain and blood flowed in streams. At the same time the bandits attacked the baggage train and completed the destruction.Yuan Shutried to retreat toShouchunbut the bandits barred the road.

He sought refuge inJiangting, with the small company left of all his army. And these were the weakly ones able neither to fight nor flee. It was then the height of summer and their food was nearly exhausted. The whole provision consisted of thirty measures of wheat. This was made over to the soldiers and the members of his household went hungry. Many died of actual starvation.Yuan Shucould not swallow the coarse food that the soldiers lived on. One day he bade his cook bring him some honey-water to quench his thirst.

“There is no water, save that tainted with blood,” replied the cook. “Where can I get honey-water?”

This was the last straw.Yuan Shusat up on his couch and rolled out on the floor with a loud cry. Blood gushed from his mouth and thus he died. It was thesixth month of the fourth year of Established Tranquility.

The last days ofHanapproached and weapons clashed in every quarter,

The misguidedYuan Shu, lost to all sense of honor,

Forgetful of his forefathers, who had filled the state’s highest offices,

Madly aspired to become himself Emperor,

Resting his outrageous claim on the possession ofThe Seal,

And arrogantly boasting that thus he fulfilled the design of Heaven.

Alas! Sick unto death he vainly begged for a little honey-water,

He died, alone.

Yuan Shubeing dead, his nephew,Yuan Yin, fled toLujiang, taking with himYuan Shu’s surviving relatives and his coffin. En route they were captured and killed by themagistrate,Xu Qiu. Among the possessions he found theHereditary Seal, which he at once took tothe capitaland presented toCao Cao, for which he was madeGrand AdministratorofJailing.

WhenXuandeheard thatYuan Shuwas dead he prepared a report to the throne, and sent it toCao Cao. He sent the two officers deputed byCao Cao,Zhu LingandLu Zhao, back tothe capital, keeping the army to defendXu province. He also personally went through the countryside commanding the people to resume their ordinary avocations.

Cao Caowas angry when his two officers returned without their man and was going to put them to death.

Xun Yureasoned with him. “The power was inLiu Bei’s hands and so these two had no alternative,” said he.

So they were pardoned.

“You should instructChe Zhouto try to destroy him,” suggestedXun Yu.

Accordingly he sent secret orders toChe Zhou, who tookChen Denginto his confidence and asked his advice.Chen Dengadvised an ambush in the city gate to attackLiu Beion his return from the country; he himself would attack the escort with arrows from the city walls.Che Zhouagreed to try this.

ThenChen Dengwent to his father to tell him. His fatherChen Guibade him go and warn the intended victim. He at once rode away to do so. Before long he met the two younger brothers, to whom he told his story.

NowXuandewas following some distance behind. As soon asZhang Feiheard of the plot he wanted to attack the ambush, butYunchangsaid he had a better plan.

Said he, “The ambush will be a failure. And I think we can compass the death ofChe Zhou. In the night we will pretend to be some ofCao Cao’s men and entice him out to meet us. We will slay him.”

Zhang Feiapproved of the plan. Now the soldiers still had some ofCao Cao’s army banners and wore similar armor. About the third watch they came to the city wall and hailed the gate. Those on guard asked who they were. The men replied that they wereZhang Wenyuan’s troop sent from the capital. This was toldChe Zhouwho sent hastily forChen Dengto ask his advice.

“If I do not receive them they will suspect my loyalty,” said he. “Yet if I go out I may be victim of a ruse.”

So he went up on the wall and said it was too dark to distinguish friends from foes and they must wait until daylight. The men shouted back thatLiu Beimust be kept in ignorance and they begged him to let them in. StillChe Zhouhesitated. They shouted louder than ever to open the gate.

PresentlyChe Zhougirded on his armor, placed himself at the head of a company and went out. He galloped over the bridge, shouting, “Where isWenyuan?”

Then lights blazed around and he recognizedGuan Yuwith his sword drawn.

“Wretch!” criedGuan Yu. “You would plot to slay my brother, would you?”

Che Zhouwas too frightened to make good defense and he turned to reenter the gate. But as he reached the drawbridge flights of arrows met him, wherefore he turned aside and galloped along under the wall. ButGuan Yucame quickly in pursuit. His sword was raised aloft and as it came down the fugitive fell to the earth.Guan Yucut off his head and returned, shouting, “I have slain the traitor. You others need not fear if you only surrender.”

They threw aside their spears and gave in. As soon as the excitement had calmedGuan Yutook the head to showXuandeand told him the story of the plot.

“But what willCao Caothink of this?” saidXuande. “And he may come.”

“If he does we can meet him,” saidGuan Yu.

ButXuandewas grieved beyond measure. When he entered the city the elders of the people knelt in the road to welcome him. When he reached his residence he found thatZhang Feihad already exterminated the family ofChe Zhou.

Xuandesaid, “We have slain one of his trusted officers. Do you think he will abide that without taking action?”

“Never mind that!” saidChen Deng. “I have a plan.”

Just from grave danger extricated,

An injured friend must be placated.

The plan proposed byChen Dengwill be disclosed next.
